title: Installment Note — First Payment Split
event: Reyes Co. borrows $60,000 on a 3-year, 8% installment note requiring equal annual payments of $23,282. Prepare the first payment entry.

entries.label: Interest first, principal takes the rest

lines.label: 60,000 × 8%
lines.why: Interest on the FULL beginning balance — the first payment is the most interest-heavy.
lines.trap: Rate × outstanding BALANCE, never rate × payment.

lines.label: 23,282 − 4,800
lines.why: Whatever the payment doesn't spend on interest reduces principal.
lines.trap: Principal is the PLUG — each year interest shrinks and this line grows; balance is now 41,518.

lines.label: level payment
lines.why: Same check every year — the internal split is what moves.
lines.trap: Equal PAYMENTS ≠ equal principal reduction; that confusion is the chapter's core trap.

memorize.body: Each period: interest = rate × beginning balance; principal = payment − interest; new balance = old − principal.
An amortization table is just this three-step loop repeated — build one row and you can build them all.

questions.prompt: $60,000 note at 8%, annual payment $23,282. Principal reduction in payment 1 = ?

distractors.feedback: That's the whole payment — 4,800 of it is interest. Only the remainder touches principal.
Dividing 60,000 by 3 assumes equal PRINCIPAL. Installment notes have equal PAYMENTS — principal grows each year.

build.scaffold: Interest from the balance, principal as the plug, cash for the level payment.
